title: An original resolution authorizing expenditures by the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs.
plain_summary: This resolution gives the Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs permission to spend about thirty million dollars over two years from March 2025 to February 2027. The committee will use this money to hire workers, pay for help from experts, and cover other costs while they do their job. During this time, they will investigate important issues like how well the government works, organized crime, energy management, and other things that affect national security. This funding allows the committee to do thorough research and investigations to help keep the country safe and running smoothly.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Rules and Administration. (text: CR S979-980)
subjects: Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs

sections.title: Section 1 General Authority
sections.text: This section gives the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs permission to do its work from March first two thousand twenty five through February twenty eighth two thousand twenty seven. The committee can spend money from the Senate's backup fund. The committee can hire staff members. The committee can also use workers from other government departments or agencies if those departments agree and if the Committee on Rules and Administration says it is okay. The committee might have to pay back the other departments for using their workers or it might not have to pay them back.

sections.title: Section 2 Expenses
sections.text: This section sets spending limits for the committee during three different time periods. For March first two thousand twenty five through September thirtieth two thousand twenty five, the committee cannot spend more than eight million three hundred eighty thousand three hundred eighty eight dollars. Of this money, no more than four hundred thousand dollars can be used to hire outside consultants or consulting companies. No more than twenty thousand dollars can be used to train the committee's professional staff members. For October first two thousand twenty five through September thirtieth two thousand twenty six, the committee cannot spend more than fourteen million three hundred sixty six thousand three hundred seventy nine dollars. The same limits apply for consultants and training. For October first two thousand twenty six through February twenty eighth two thousand twenty seven, the committee cannot spend more than five million nine hundred eighty five thousand nine hundred ninety one dollars. Again, the same limits apply for consultants and training.

sections.title: Section 3 Expenses and Agency Contributions
sections.text: This section explains how the committee will pay for things and what expenses do not need special approval. The committee's expenses will be paid from the Senate's backup fund when the committee chairman approves the payment vouchers (official requests for payment). However, the committee does not need vouchers for certain regular expenses. These include paying yearly salaries to employees, paying for phone and communication services from the Senate's Sergeant at Arms office, buying office supplies through the Senate's supply keeper, paying the Senate postmaster, paying for copying machine use, paying for Senate recording and photography services, and paying for mailed materials sent by the Sergeant at Arms. The section also says that money can be taken from a special Senate account called Expenses of Inquiries and Investigations to help pay for committee employee benefits during all three time periods mentioned in Section 2.

sections.title: Section 4 Investigations
sections.text: This section gives the committee broad power to study and investigate many different areas. The committee can look into how efficiently and economically all parts of the government operate. This includes finding fraud, wrongdoing, bad management, corruption, unethical behavior, waste, overspending, conflicts of interest, and improper use of government money. The committee can investigate problems between government workers and companies that do business with the government. The committee can study labor union and employer relations to see if any criminal or harmful activities are hurting the public, employers, or workers. The committee can investigate organized crime that uses interstate or international commerce and how criminals have gotten into legitimate businesses. The committee can look into all kinds of crime and lawlessness in the United States that affects national health, welfare, and safety, including investment fraud, commodity fraud, security fraud, computer fraud, and the use of offshore banking to commit crimes. The committee can study how well national security methods and processes work and whether they make good use of the nation's knowledge and talents. The committee can investigate how government agencies handle energy shortages and controls, including fuel statistics, conservation measures, energy pricing, coordination with state and local governments, fuel exports, tax and import policies, competition in the petroleum industry, fuel allocation, government energy supplies, relations with other oil producing countries, compliance monitoring, and research into alternative energy sources. The committee can study how well federal regulatory policies and programs work. When doing these investigations, the committee is not limited to looking only at government records and can examine the records and activities of any person, corporation, or other organization. The committee has special powers to do this work, including the ability to subpoena witnesses and documents, hold hearings, meet at any time during Senate sessions or breaks, administer oaths, and take testimony either spoken or written. Committee staff members can also take depositions according to committee rules. This section does not take away any powers from other Senate committees. All subpoena powers that the committee had under a previous resolution from the one hundred eighteenth Congress are allowed to continue.
